content.text: A GenAI platform team of five cannot anticipate every use case across fifty product teams. Inner-sourcing extends the platform by letting product engineers contribute new provider adapters, scorer implementations, prompt templates, or gateway middleware — with the same review rigour as a closed platform team, but with a clear, documented path that any engineer can follow. This model is not open contribution with no gatekeeping: it is structured contribution with trusted committers who enforce quality, security, and architectural consistency before anything merges.
Roles in the Inner-Source Model
Role
Who fills it
Responsibilities
Trusted Committer (TC)
Platform team engineers; up to 2 experienced contributors promoted per year
Merge PRs, enforce contribution standards, mentor contributors, define roadmap, cut releases
Contributor
Any product team engineer
Propose features via issues, implement against the contribution guide, respond to review feedback, write tests
Component Owner
Named in CODEOWNERS for a sub-module
Primary reviewer for changes to their component; does not have merge rights by default
Product Owner
Platform team lead
Prioritise the inner-source backlog, decide which contributions fit the roadmap, resolve disputes
Trusted Committer vs. Contributor:

A TC is not just a reviewer — they are accountable for what ships. They must understand the downstream impact of a change on all consumers of the library. A contributor can be excellent but narrow; a TC needs the breadth to evaluate whether a new provider adapter, for example, creates an abstraction leak that breaks the existing middleware pipeline.
Repository Structure for Inner-Source
Each platform repository follows a standard layout that signals to contributors exactly where to look. The most important file for a first-time contributor is
CONTRIBUTING.md
— it must be accurate, current, and short enough to read in ten minutes.

genai-llm-sdk/
├── CONTRIBUTING.md        # how to contribute — the first file a contributor reads
├── CODEOWNERS             # maps paths to review owners
├── GOVERNANCE.md          # TC election, promotion criteria, decision-making process
├── CHANGELOG.md           # auto-generated from conventional commits
├── src/
│   ├── core/              # LLMClient, PromptRef, middleware — TC-only touch zone
│   ├── adapters/          # OpenAI, Anthropic, Azure, Bedrock — contributor-friendly
│   └── scorers/           # Scorer implementations — most contributor additions land here
├── tests/
│   ├── unit/
│   ├── integration/       # run against real endpoints in CI (uses test API keys from Vault)
│   └── contract/          # pact tests — ensure adapters meet the LLMProvider protocol
└── docs/
    ├── adr/               # Architecture Decision Records
    └── guides/            # contributor how-to guides
CONTRIBUTING.md Structure

The contribution guide must answer five questions before a contributor writes a line of code: where to start, how to set up, how to test, how the review works, and how long it takes. A guide that skips any of these causes a support request to the TC instead.

# Contributing to genai-llm-sdk

## Before you start
Open a GitHub issue describing what you want to add or fix.
A Trusted Committer will confirm it fits the roadmap within 3 business days.
This avoids building something that won't be accepted.

## Setup
```bash
git clone https://github.com/internal/genai-llm-sdk
cd genai-llm-sdk
python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -e ".[dev]"
pre-commit install
```

## Adding a new provider adapter
1. Create `src/adapters/<provider>.py` — implement the `LLMProvider` protocol.
2. Add unit tests in `tests/unit/adapters/test_<provider>.py`.
3. Add a contract test in `tests/contract/` that verifies the adapter satisfies `LLMProvider`.
4. Update `src/adapters/__init__.py` to export the new adapter.
5. Add a row to `docs/adapters.md`.

## Tests
```bash
pytest tests/unit/                    # fast; runs without network
pytest tests/contract/                # requires PROVIDER_API_KEY in env
```

## Pull request checklist
- [ ] Issue linked in PR description
- [ ] All unit tests pass
- [ ] Contract test added for new adapter
- [ ] `CHANGELOG.md` entry added under `[Unreleased]`
- [ ] No secrets, keys, or internal URLs in the diff
- [ ] DCO sign-off (`git commit -s`)

## Review timeline
Trusted Committers aim to give initial feedback within 5 business days.
Two TC approvals are required to merge.
DCO and Developer Certificate of Origin
The platform uses the Developer Certificate of Origin (DCO) rather than a CLA. Every commit must carry a
Signed-off-by
trailer, which the contributor adds with
git commit -s
. The CI DCO bot rejects any PR with unsigned commits. This is a lightweight legal acknowledgement that the contributor has the right to contribute the code.

# Sign off a commit
git commit -s -m "feat(adapters): add Bedrock Claude adapter"

# The commit message will contain:
# Signed-off-by: Jane Doe <jane.doe@company.com>

# If you forgot to sign a previous commit:
git commit --amend -s --no-edit

# For multiple commits, use interactive rebase to add sign-offs:
git rebase HEAD~3 --signoff
CODEOWNERS and Review Routing
GitHub's
CODEOWNERS

file routes review requests automatically. The platform team owns the core middleware; component owners are product team engineers who have been delegated ownership of a specific adapter or scorer they wrote. This gives contributors a clear path from writing code to having meaningful review authority over the module they maintain.

# CODEOWNERS — auto-assigns reviewers based on changed paths

# Core middleware: TC-only review
/src/core/              @platform-ai/trusted-committers

# Adapters: component owner + at least 1 TC
/src/adapters/openai.py     @jane-doe @platform-ai/trusted-committers
/src/adapters/anthropic.py  @john-smith @platform-ai/trusted-committers
/src/adapters/bedrock.py    @alice-jones @platform-ai/trusted-committers

# Scorers: component owner + 1 TC
/src/scorers/             @platform-ai/trusted-committers

# Docs: any TC
/docs/                  @platform-ai/trusted-committers

# Release scripts: TC-only
/.github/workflows/release.yml  @platform-ai/trusted-committers
CI Pipeline for Inner-Source PRs
The CI pipeline enforces quality before any human reviewer sees the code. A PR that fails CI is not reviewed — this saves TC time and teaches contributors to use the pipeline as fast feedback.

# .github/workflows/pr.yml
name: PR checks
on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install -e ".[dev]"
      - run: ruff check src/ tests/
      - run: mypy src/ --strict

  dco:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with: {fetch-depth: 0}
      - uses: dco-check/dco-check@v3

  security:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: returntocorp/semgrep-action@v1
        with:
          config: >-
            p/python
            p/secrets
            p/owasp-top-ten

  unit-tests:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install -e ".[dev]"
      - run: pytest tests/unit/ --cov=src --cov-fail-under=85

  contract-tests:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    env:
      OPENAI_API_KEY:    ${{ secrets.TEST_OPENAI_KEY }}
      ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.TEST_ANTHROPIC_KEY }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install -e ".[dev]"
      - run: pytest tests/contract/ -x
Contributor Promotion to Trusted Committer

A contributor who makes four or more accepted contributions to the same module within twelve months is eligible for nomination to Trusted Committer for that module. Promotion is not automatic — the existing TCs vote and must reach consensus. The criteria are:
Code quality: contributions consistently required few review rounds and no security findings.
Testing discipline: contributed tests caught regressions before they reached production at least once.
Responsiveness: addressed review feedback within five business days on average.
Community: helped other contributors in GitHub discussions or the platform Slack channel.
Scope readiness: demonstrates understanding of the full module, not just the features they contributed.
A Trusted Committer who becomes inactive (no review activity for six months) is moved to Emeritus status. They retain read access and are acknowledged in the repo but no longer have merge rights.
Release Process After a Contribution Merges
The platform uses conventional commits to drive automated changelog generation and semver bumping. A TC triggers the release by pushing a version tag; GitHub Actions does the rest.

# Conventional commit types used in this repo:
# feat:     new feature → MINOR version bump
# fix:      bug fix → PATCH bump
# feat!:    breaking change → MAJOR bump
# docs:     documentation only → no release
# test:     test only → no release
# chore:    maintenance → no release

# Example commit messages:
git commit -s -m "feat(adapters): add AWS Bedrock Claude adapter"
git commit -s -m "fix(core): retry on 429 before raising QuotaExceeded"
git commit -s -m "feat!: rename LLMProvider.complete() to LLMProvider.chat()"

# TC triggers a release:
git tag v2.3.0
git push origin v2.3.0
# → GitHub Actions runs: changelog gen, PyPI publish, Slack announce,
#   Backstage catalog update, auto-PR to pin consumers to v2.3.0
Common Pitfalls for Inner-Source Programs
Pitfall
Symptom
Fix
No issue required before PR
TC receives PRs for features that conflict with the roadmap; contributor effort wasted
Enforce: CI bot adds a label requiring a linked issue before a TC will review
TC bottleneck (1 person)
PRs wait 2+ weeks; contributors give up and fork instead
Maintain at least 2 active TCs per repository; review load is shared and no single absence blocks the queue
CONTRIBUTING.md out of date
Contributors follow stale setup steps, hit errors on day one, abandon the attempt
CONTRIBUTING.md is a living doc owned by the TCs; update it as part of any tooling change PR
No security scan in CI
A contributed adapter includes a hardcoded API key that passes code review
Semgrep or similar scanner blocks merge; secrets never reach main
No recognition for contributors
Contribution count is invisible; motivation drops after the first merge
Auto-generate a CONTRIBUTORS file on release; mention contributors in the Slack release note; track in the platform scorecard
End-to-End Walkthrough: Adding a Bedrock Claude Adapter
Walking through a concrete contribution makes the process tangible. This example adds an AWS Bedrock adapter to
genai-llm-sdk
— a common request from teams deploying in AWS-only environments.
1. Open an Issue

**Title:** feat(adapters): AWS Bedrock Claude adapter

**Description:**
Our team runs in a VPC with no outbound internet access. We need a Bedrock
adapter so we can use Claude through the AWS endpoint without routing through
the public Anthropic API.

**Proposed interface:**
```python
from genai_sdk.adapters import BedrockAdapter
adapter = BedrockAdapter(region="us-east-1", model_id="anthropic.claude-haiku-4-5")
client  = LLMClient(provider=adapter)
```

**I'm willing to implement this.** Estimated effort: 3 days.
A TC responds within 3 business days: confirms the feature fits the roadmap, links the
LLMProvider
protocol docs, and adds the
help-wanted
and
adapter
labels. This is the contributor's green light to start.
2. Implement the Adapter
3. Write Contract and Unit Tests

# tests/contract/test_bedrock_contract.py
import pytest
from genai_sdk.adapters.bedrock import BedrockAdapter
from genai_sdk.core.protocol import CompletionRequest

@pytest.mark.skipif(
    not os.environ.get("TEST_BEDROCK_REGION"),
    reason="Requires AWS credentials and TEST_BEDROCK_REGION"
)
def test_bedrock_satisfies_llmprovider_contract():
    adapter = BedrockAdapter(
        region=os.environ["TEST_BEDROCK_REGION"],
        model_id="anthropic.claude-haiku-4-5-20251001-v1:0",
    )
    req  = CompletionRequest(user="Say 'hello' and nothing else.", max_tokens=10)
    resp = adapter.complete(req)

    assert isinstance(resp.text, str) and len(resp.text) > 0
    assert resp.input_tokens  > 0
    assert resp.output_tokens > 0
    assert resp.provider == "bedrock"

# tests/unit/adapters/test_bedrock.py
def test_supports_model_accepts_anthropic_prefix():
    adapter = BedrockAdapter.__new__(BedrockAdapter)  # skip __init__
    assert adapter.supports_model("anthropic.claude-haiku-4-5") is True
    assert adapter.supports_model("amazon.titan-text-v2")       is False
4. Open the Pull Request

**PR title:** feat(adapters): add AWS Bedrock Claude adapter

**Linked issue:** #142

**Summary:**
Implements `BedrockAdapter` using `boto3.client("bedrock-runtime")`.
Satisfies the `LLMProvider` protocol contract.
No outbound internet required — calls stay within the VPC via the
Bedrock VPC endpoint.

**Test coverage:**
- Unit tests: `tests/unit/adapters/test_bedrock.py` (no network required)
- Contract test: `tests/contract/test_bedrock_contract.py` (requires `TEST_BEDROCK_REGION`)

**Checklist:**
- [x] Issue linked
- [x] Unit tests pass (`pytest tests/unit/`)
- [x] Contract test passes (tested locally with sandbox credentials)
- [x] CHANGELOG.md updated under [Unreleased]
- [x] `docs/adapters.md` row added
- [x] DCO sign-off on all commits

Signed-off-by: Alice Jones <alice.jones@company.com>

Two Trusted Committers review within 5 business days. They request one change: the token count estimate should be documented as approximate. Alice adds a docstring, pushes the fix, and the PR is merged. On the next release day the TC tags
and the adapter ships to all consumers.
Inner-Source Backlog Management
The platform maintains a public inner-source backlog — a GitHub Project board visible to all engineers. Issues labelled
inner-source-welcome
are pre-scoped by a TC, confirmed to fit the roadmap, and estimated. This removes the biggest friction point: contributors don't have to guess whether their idea will be accepted.
Label
Meaning
Who can pick it up
inner-source-welcome
Scoped, roadmap-approved, implementation path documented
Any engineer; TC available to answer questions
good-first-contribution
Small, self-contained change; ideal for first-time contributors
Any engineer; no prior platform knowledge required
help-wanted
TC has started but is blocked or too busy; existing work available
Engineer with relevant domain expertise
tc-only
Touches core middleware or release pipeline; contributor PRs will not be accepted
TCs only
needs-design
Feature requested but implementation path unclear; RFC discussion needed first
Senior contributors or TCs
Summary

The inner-source model for GenAI platform tools extends the platform team's reach without losing quality control. Trusted Committers set the bar — they enforce the contribution guide, run the review gate, and cut releases — while contributors from product teams add adapters, scorers, and prompt templates that the platform team would never have time to build. The model works when three things are in place: a CONTRIBUTING.md that a new contributor can follow without asking for help, a CI pipeline that catches issues before human review, and a TC pool deep enough that no single person's absence blocks the queue. Promotion from contributor to TC gives long-term contributors a meaningful stake in the tools they depend on.
